speaker: interviewer
text: Of our interview with Clara Heller Isaacman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, who was this group at the party?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a birthday party of one of the German soldiers. There is only one civilian there. On my right hand side you can see it-- you can see him. He is von Oster. He's the one who worked as a spy for the Belgium army and also saved over 100 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken during the war. It was given to me in Antwerp. A museum gave me a copy of it, or the original. I-- I have the original too. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a memorial postcard for a soldier who gave his life for liberation of Belgium, and who also saved Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew his parents. I really don't remember seeing him, but I know what he did. And his parents gave me this after he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: The back of the memorial card reads, 'Mr. Meyer Constant Scheepers-Rampart.' And the date of death is October 2nd, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Antwerp, a few hours after I went out from Stadionstraat after we were liberated. My sister was blond. She was able to get her hair blond with peroxide during hiding, because she wanted to go outside. I couldn't do it. I tried to, but I couldn't. Now, no trouble. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a Israeli who worked with the Jewish brigade during the war. England gave the Israeli a separate army. And they-- they gave him permission to work, and liberate, and work with the Jews who survived. I worked with him in Antwerp with concentration camp Jews, who came back from concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Albert. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Hold it one minute. Holtzman. Albert Holtzman. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is in the streets of Antwerp after the liberation. My mother and I. I think this was our first outfit that was ours. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The first time Blancha met my Denny, and it's in the back, in the yard where we were hidden on Stadionstraat. As you can see it's a tall wall. The neighbors could never see us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Blancha's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-- von Oste. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is after the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Michael Adams, where I was hidden, and myself. In the backyard you can see the little roof. That was the house where we were hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this group of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This group of people are the people who worked for the American army at the depot. And I'm in the front. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there too. I worked as a typist and translator.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anybody you can identify in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: This lady is Blancha von Oster. And she's walking in the streets of Antwerp after the war. And she came and gave it to me very proudly. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is another soldier of the Jewish brigade who lives now in Tel Aviv and is a engineer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He gave me this picture not long ago, maybe five years ago. He too worked in Antwerp with the survivors. And I worked with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Schuman. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Those are two of my teachers from Antwerp before the war. I'm sorry I don't remember their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which school was this? The public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Excuse me. This was the pass to get into the depot. As you see it has the United States stamp, the serial number, and everything. I was a soldier for the United States, after the war of course. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Anika's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] house [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Anika's last name during the war? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She never told me. We just knew her--

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: We just knew her-- Noble, as in the-- after the war. And she is sitting. The young lady who has the hand on her is her daughter Sarah. Then it's Kroll, and then it's Blancha. Blanca died a year after this picture was taken. Kroll is still alive. He's in a home. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This is my wedding picture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I couldn't have had a wedding without the help of the surviving Jews in Antwerp. My dress was made-- I would say each Jew had once put in the needle to put it together. And there wasn't a Jew who didn't come to my wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, July 24th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the gentleman in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is my husband, Daniel Isaacman. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We had to have two weddings. One was a Jewish wedding and one was-- the same day, we had to go to the Stadthaus. And this is at the Stadthaus. As you can see, the guards are Belgium guards and we're walking in to be married to officially in-- as Belgium, in the Stadthaus. And then the [INAUDIBLE], all the other things were in a different place. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As you see, there's a picture the Stadthaus and Monument of the Unknown Soldier. And the guards were the-- my hands again-- the American-- I had an American honor guard, my husband's friends and the doctors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The best man-- my best man was von Oster, and here he is in uniform. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We are being sworn in, the Americans have-- as witnesses. One-- both of them were his doctors, the doctors from the unit. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Danny as a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Antwerp. I think it was taken before we got married. Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken after the war. And I remember very well that this was my first hairdo that a beautician worked on me after three years of never having a haircut by anybody but my mother or whatever. And I felt very good about it. Of course, also sad as you see. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is my first picture with Danny Isaacman. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This picture was taken a few hours before I left to take the boat to come to the United States of America. Near me is my brother, Elie Heller, my mother, Rose Heller, Frieda Heller. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] When I met my husband, he had a good friend, Ruby Gorowitz. And this is a picture of my sister, who became my sister's husband later, and Danny and I. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This picture [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] is very dear to me because the man that's standing near my sister, Chaim, had just come back from Auschwitz, from the concentration camp. He told us about my brother. But he told it to us that my mother shouldn't hear. He died a few months later from leukemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 19-- end of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he tell you about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told us that my brother could have maybe lived if he would have wanted to cooperate with the Germans, if he would have want to become a capo. My brother refused, and he didn't live too long after they brought him in to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember Chaim's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just know him by Chaim. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This picture my husband sent to me after I remained in Antwerp and he came home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's his uncle that's near him. [? Chaimwitz ?] is his name and it happened to be also Chaim [? Chaimwitz. ?] And his mother, Esther Isaacman, and Ruben Isaacman. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] When my cou-- my husband became president of Gratz College [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1972. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in the-- at the occasion of his becoming president.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Do you want to introduce the gentleman on the right to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Of course. It's my only child, my dear son, Jonathan Isaacman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Gratz College.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Philadelphia. I'm so tired. [LAUGHS] This picture means also an awful lot to me, like every picture, of course. Elie Wiesel, who encouraged me to write my first book, and who was a good friend of ours and still is. When my book was finished, he had signing books his own. But when I went over to him and I said, I brought mine, he said, of course, Clara. And he's signing my book. And this ha-- this was taken at Beth Shalom. He spoke at Beth Shalom. And my husband already was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1996 we're now. 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was at Beth Shalom in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Elkins Park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pennsylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pennsylvania. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Isaacman, who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the groups that I used to take to Israel every summer from Gratz College. They were students-- high school students-- who applied to study in Israel and also to travel. We were in Israel for six and a half weeks every summer with professors. And we traveled and hiked all over Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was taken in [INAUDIBLE], near Haifa, where we were staying for four weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we traveled all over the Negev and we stayed one week in Jerusalem. And as a matter of fact, we had a stopover in Rome before we got to Israel, and we visited the Vatican. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a window at Gratz College library. When my husband passed away, they put together what he did during a short life. As you can see, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he had worked, of course, for Gratz a lot, but also for the rest of the Jewish people. One of the citations there are from the federation, fom the [INAUDIBLE]. The other one is a dinner with Falkenheim, one of the most renowned people in the world, who wrote And so on and so forth. I'm not going to enumerate everything, but this was my husband. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The next one is a card that I just received from Antwerp, from the Addams family. And I was very much surprised to receive it. And it's a Merry Christmas and Happy New Year card.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it says inside, 'Dear Clara and family. Wish you a very happy new year in 1997, and a very prosperous, long time and peace in the world. Your Richard and family.'

speaker: interviewee
text: The next one is when it existed. And the date is there when I arrived to Philadelphia. And there is an article of the boat who brought us here and telling the names of the brides who came specifically to Philadelphia. And one of them was myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date on that is March 15, 1946. And three liners bringing more war brides, and listed in underlined is Clara Heller Isaacman who was going to South 5th Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next one is the [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] passport that I spoke so much about, and that really saved our lives a few times. It's my mother's passport. And in each section, of course, one tells her birthday, the other one Romanian, and the date when it's valid, and so on. This piece-- it was four pieces of paper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It meant a lot, and still mean a lot to me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My Jewish star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the beginning when I wore it, I was proud of it. I didn't mind. I was a Jew. And I couldn't care less if the world saw me wearing a star. A little bit later after the occupation, the Germans, the SS, the Hitler Jugend, if they passed us-- or if they passed me or my friend-- and saw the Jewish star on us, and we couldn't walk without it-- they used to spit at us, push us. And we were afraid. We got to be afraid wearing this Jewish star. So I tried to hide it with a book and with many other things. I wore it until I went into hiding. And then I put it in my photo album. And that's how I have it. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] In 1992, the summer of 1992, I was invited to the Jagiellonian University in Krakow, one of the renowned universities in Europe and in the world, to be a lecturer and guide to international students. I was the only one who didn't have professorship. They brought in fluent professors from all over the world. I spent, with international students, for four weeks in Poland. I traveled to little towns where 40, 50 Jews-- 40,000, 50,000 Jews were killed, until we ended up in Auschwitz and in Birkenau where I lectured. And then they decided to take my book and they made out of the cover, a picture that is in one of the rooms there. And the book is in the library. This is just a notice that I gave them permission to do it, and that they did it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the stories that I would like to tell is that we got into a little town [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- where there were 50,000 Jews killed. And we were met by an old man who took us to his home. His home was a small little house. And when we walked in there, the 20 of us, there was no room for us even to sit on the floor. A few of us had to have the door open to have enough room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And through interpreter-- through our interpreter-- this man spoke only Polish-- he said to us, where do you come from? And we told him, from the United States, from Israel, from there, from there. He said, no, that's not what I mean. Where did you park your bus? We said, in a parking place not too far from here. He said, what did you see there? And I have to admit none of us saw anything. He said, come with me. We went back. And right near the bus was a stone carved out in memory of the 60,000 Jews who lived in this town, written in Hebrew and Yiddish and in English. And it was very dirty. And he said, see this? He said, I-- I fought for 20 years that they should let me put up a memory, a stone, for those Jews. I was here when they shot the Jews all down on this little place. He said, and there was a little child who was shouting, water, water, I want a drink of water. And I went home, where you just came from, and I brought a pitcher of water. And one of the German soldiers said, what are you doing? And I said to him, I'd like to give the child his last wish. And he said, if you give him the water, we'll shoot you too.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I went home. And this is my memory for all of those kids. He said, the only thing that bothers me is that it's so dirty and nobody ever comes. Making a long story short, my group and I swept and brushed and cleaned up the stone, and we said Kaddish. And the whole town came around us. This is only one little vignette of my four weeks in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Isaacman, will you tell us about the book that's at Auschwitz, in the Auschwitz library?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's called That means, years of anxiety. It's the same book that is It was translated in Belgium, and it's being used in Belgium and Holland in the school systems. They're now trying to write it and translate it in French.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the book written? When was it first published?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was published [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1989. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they are using it a lot. It's in the second edition. is in the fifth edition. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About whatever I spoke, it's part in the book. But it was written more for younger children, so there are many things that I did not tell in the book. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote it because I hope that I will teach with it, not just read it. And I think that people who will see the video will get to know about

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this story about-- this book, I should say?

speaker: interviewee
text: This book is called What I have in this book is a story about an SS man who is telling his story and letting us judge if it was right or wrong what he did. I also have in this book a story of a Dr. Leo Bass, a black man who liberated Buchenwald. And the rest of the stories here are true stories-- not that the other two are not-- of eyewitnesses, people who were there and who are being interviewed by Shoah now. And who, their stories, they will tell themselves too, their-- with their own voice. I translated it.
